item.title: Assisted dying bill will not now become law, say both sides
item.description: The House of Lords are continuing to debate the proposals but there is not enough time to complete all the stages in the current parliamentary session.

item.title: Iran War Live Updates: Trump Claims Progress in Talks and Extends Strait of Hormuz Deadline
item.description: President Trump has threatened to attack Iran’s power plants unless it fully reopens the Strait of Hormuz, a vital oil route, by April 6. Israel on Friday launched more strikes on Iran.

item.title: Morocco ready to host Wafcon 'any time' as questions remain over postponement
item.description: A senior member of the Moroccan government says the country was “ready to host” the Women’s Africa Cup of Nations this month despite the decision to delay it until July.

item.title: Pernod Ricard and Jack Daniel’s owner in talks to combine operations
item.description: Discussions under way with Brown-Forman to create premium alcohol group as sector battles subdued demand

item.title: Rationing power and diluting petrol - how African countries are coping with effects of Iran war
item.description: The disruption in oil supplies has caused shortages and concerns in several African countries.

item.title: Vital paperwork for grooming gangs inquiry may be lost, says MP
item.description: An independent inquiry into child sexual abuse by grooming gangs is about to begin its work.

item.title: Marco Rubio meets G7 counterparts amid Iran war
item.description: It is the secretary of state's first foreign trip since the conflict began last month.

item.title: U.K. Police to Reinvestigate Sex Crime Allegations Against Andrew Tate
item.description: A police force in England said it would look again at allegations against the influencer from 11 years ago. Mr. Tate was not charged in the case and denies any wrongdoing.

item.title: British parents told to limit screen time for under-5s to one hour a day
item.description: UK issues guidance while drawing up options to restrict children’s use of social media

item.title: Ukraine signs deal with Saudi Arabia offering drone expertise
item.description: Volodymyr Zelensky says the Gulf kingdom faces the same type of attacks from Iran that Kyiv has been resisting.

item.title: Putin asks oligarchs to donate to budget as cost of Ukraine war soars
item.description: Russian president’s demand shows intention to pursue campaign until all of Donbas region is captured

item.title: Rubio Expected to Press Allies Over Strait of Hormuz at G7 Meeting
item.description: The meeting in France is slated to discuss efforts to stop the war in the Middle East, end Iran’s nuclear and ballistic missile development, and reopen maritime trade routes.

item.title: UAE and Qatar Arrest Hundreds Over Online Videos of Iranian Attacks
item.description: The authorities have made a wave of arrests to stop people posting footage of strikes, citing security risks. Experts also see a fear of damage to the countries’ image as safe havens.

item.title: A tourist tax is a better way to fund museums
item.description: Plans to charge UK residents to visit cultural institutions may not deliver the financial boost they promise

item.title: I joined an Easter procession for the fitness... and stayed for the faith
item.description: Holy Week in Cadiz has helped one writer reconnect with himself and his community

item.title: Italy probes Sephora over fears marketing is driving ‘cosmeticorexia’ in girls
item.description: Competition regulator claims retailer and Benefit Cosmetics encourage purchases through ‘covert’ social media marketing

item.title: Screen time for under-fives should be limited to one hour a day, parents told
item.description: New government guidance also suggests children under-two should not be watching screens alone.

item.title: Whale swims for freedom after big German rescue effort on Baltic coast
item.description: Rescue teams now believe the whale has reached deeper water in Lübeck Bay and hope it will head for the wider sea.

item.title: Europeans Are Angry at Trump, but Often Forgiving of Americans
item.description: A generation ago, foreign fury over the Iraq invasion often blurred into anti-Americanism. Now, some Europeans seem ready to distinguish between the president and the American people.

item.title: Resurgent Inflation Tests Faith in Fed’s Willingness to Tame It
item.description: The war in the Middle East risks worsening an inflation problem that the Federal Reserve has struggled for years to subdue.

item.title: U.S. May Label Brazilian Gangs as Terror Groups, After Push by the Bolsonaros
item.description: The Trump administration is weighing declaring Brazil’s two largest drug gangs terrorist groups, after lobbying by the sons of jailed former President Jair Bolsonaro.

item.title: Lee Geun-an, Infamous ‘Torture Master’ Under South Korean Dictator, Dies at 88
item.description: The name of Mr. Lee, a former police inspector, had long incited fear and hatred in the country.

item.title: South Africa's exclusion from G7 summit no surprise, says Ramaphosa
item.description: Reports say the US pressured France to rescind Ramaphosa’s invitation after Trump threatened to boycott the summit.

item.title: Officials to ask Mandelson to hand over personal phone messages for release
item.description: Officials are set to publish more documents related to his appointment as UK ambassador to the US - but have so far only had access to the peer's work phone.

item.title: Two hereditary peers allowed to keep ceremonial roles
item.description: The Duke of Norfolk and Lord Carrington won the concession due to their role in organising state occasions.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
Women secretly filmed, then ridiculed and abused online
item.description: Women in Kenya and Ghana tell the BBC about being approached by a Russian man who later posts videos of them without their consent.

item.title: Nepal swears in ex-rapper as new prime minister
item.description: Balendra Shah won the election by a landslide promising change to Nepalis who are angry at corruption.

item.title: Nepal’s New Prime Minister, Former Rapper Balendra Shah, Sworn In
item.description: A leaked report on the deadly violence and mass arson that broke out last year has put the new leader under pressure to ensure accountability.

item.title: Singapore denies Malaysian scholar entry, calling her 'undesirable visitor'
item.description: Fadiah Nadwa Fikri urged some in Singapore to "adopt her brand of radical advocacy", authorities say.

item.title: Nato allies to train with Ukraine as Europe seeks closer defence ties
item.description: Also in this newsletter: Europe races to fund fusion

item.title: How Russia's threat has seen Germany become Europe's most important army
item.description: Germany once terrorised Europe, but now Europe is welcoming a resurgent German military.

item.title: China scales new heights with world’s longest outdoor escalator
item.description: Mountainous region of Chongqing is known for pushing the bounds of architecture

item.title: Trump extends Iran talks deadline after sell-off on Wall Street
item.description: US stocks post worst day since Middle East crisis began as fresh inflation fears send Treasury yields higher

item.title: The Faroe Islands, Wary After Greenland, Vote for Change
item.description: Statehood had been a key issue in this tiny Danish archipelago before President Trump threatened Greenland. Now, Faroese voters are focused more on their own economy than geopolitics.

item.title: The new Disney CEO’s horrible first week
item.description: Josh D’Amaro faces crises across the entertainment giant’s empire

item.title: UK ministers to push ahead with capped ‘mandation’ powers on pension schemes
item.description: Draft law was rejected by the House of Lords but government is planning to table a compromise after Easter

item.title: Corebridge looks to crash Apollo’s party
item.description: Insurers Equitable and Corebridge are set to merge to create a $22bn life insurance giant

item.title: Reform UK figures braced for electoral damage from Trump links
item.description: Populist party’s election campaign already hit by scandals around candidate selection

item.title: The global food crisis unleashed by the war
item.description: From Minnesota to Punjab, fertiliser costs are up and harvests are set to be hit

item.title: How Iran is seeking to cash in on Strait of Hormuz
item.description: Tehran suggests system of charging fees and limiting passage to ‘non-hostile’ ships could endure beyond the war

item.title: Ships forgo cargo to carry fuel as Iran war sends prices soaring
item.description: Fuel costs for global shipping industry are up nearly $5bn since conflict began

item.title: How MI5 gets into the minds of young terrorists
item.description: Behavioural scientists see an increasing caseload of children at risk of being radicalised online

item.title: Keir Starmer set to make Sadiq Khan a lord
item.description: Move would allow London mayor to take cabinet role, as prime minister tries to shore up position

item.title: UAE pushes for international force to reopen Hormuz
item.description: Abu Dhabi is hardening its stance as it suffers from Iran’s retaliation to US-Israeli war

item.title: Here’s What Happened in the War in the Middle East on Thursday
item.description: For a second time, President Trump extended the deadline for Iran to fully reopen the Strait of Hormuz.

item.title: In Sleepy Town on Strait of Hormuz, War Rages Just Over Horizon
item.description: For centuries, an Omani exclave has been defined by a peculiar duality: rugged isolation and proximity to one of the world’s most important trade routes.

item.title: The German Military Tightens Its Social Media Rules
item.description: Military members sharing clips on Instagram and TikTok have helped recruit badly needed new soldiers, but Bundeswehr officials said they are concerned about security.

item.title: Belarus leader gifts rifle to North Korea's Kim as they sign friendship treaty
item.description: Two key supporters of Russia's war in Ukraine close ranks as Alexander Lukashenko visits Pyongyang.

item.title: How the Hormuz closure could affect food, medicines and smartphones
item.description: The price of a wide range of goods - from food, to smartphones, to medicines - could be affected by the US-Israel war with Iran.

item.title: Olympic Committee Bars Transgender Athletes From Women’s Events
item.description: The decision is the most significant since Kirsty Coventry was elected last year to serve as president of the I.O.C.

item.title: Italy Says It Recovered $23 Million Stolen From Ursula Andress, First ‘Bond Girl’
item.description: The Swiss actress Ursula Andress, whose breakthrough came in “Dr. No” in 1962, accused a former wealth manager of embezzlement. He died in an apparent suicide last year.

item.title: Donald Trump Is Breaking Up with Europe
item.description: And the war in Iran is helping him do it.

item.title: Farage backs election candidate appearing to do Nazi salute in photo
item.description: Farage says he would not approve of the picture but would not kick him out of the party.

item.title: More than 13 million living in poverty, new figures show
item.description: The level rose slightly on the previous year while the number of children living in poverty has remained steady.

item.title: Farage says local elections are referendum on Starmer, as he launches campaign
item.description: Reform UK leader Nigel Farage is launching his party's push to take control of more councils at a rally in Sunderland.

item.title: Spanish woman dies by euthanasia after long legal battle with father
item.description: The European Court of Human Rights (ECHR) ruled in Noelia Castillo's favour earlier this week.

item.title: Liberal Democrats launch new maternity care pledge
item.description: Leader of the Liberal Democrats, Sir Ed Davey, says his proposal will be "challenging".

item.title: 'Dedicated' former MP dies aged 92
item.description: David Winnick is described as a "true legend" and "dedicated" public servant following his death.

item.title: US prosecutors argue Maduro 'plundered' Venezuelan wealth in court battle over legal fees
item.description: A judge said the right to defence was paramount as prosecutors argued the couple should not be able to use Venezuelan funds to pay lawyers.

item.title: Hungary Opens Espionage Case Against Journalist
item.description: The government of Prime Minister Viktor Orban has pursued an intensifying campaign against critics in a tough race before April elections.

item.title: Fifa investigates Congolese FA boss jailed alongside wife and son
item.description: Fecofoot president Jean-Guy Mayolas remains on the run after being sentenced to life imprisonment earlier this month for embezzling $1.1m in Fifa funds.

item.title: Australia Temporarily Blocks Iranians With Visitor Visas From Entry
item.description: Iranian tourists with visas will be barred for six months in case they are ‘unable or unlikely’ to go back because of the war, Australian officials said.

item.title: Why did US and Israel attack Iran and how long could the war last?
item.description: Conflict has spread across the Middle East since the US and Israel launched strikes on Iran on 28 February.

item.title: Orban's Hungarian government accused of mass voter intimidation ahead of election
item.description: A film quoting voters, mayors and a police officer alleges money and drugs are being offered to pressure people to back the ruling party.

item.title: Rescuers attempt to save stranded humpback whale off German coast
item.description: The marine mammal, which is stuck on a sandbank, was first spotted in Lübeck Bay on Monday.

item.title: UK now 'ready' to seize Russian shadow fleet ships, Healey says
item.description: Forces can now board sanctioned vessels if required, after ministers identified a legal basis in January.

item.title: NS&I boss replaced as savers left waiting for millions of pounds
item.description: Pensions Minister Torston Bell said those affected would receive compensation "where appropriate".

item.title: Cuban Patients Are Dying Because of U.S. Blockade, Doctors Say
item.description: Cuban health care was once the pride of the island. Now the U.S. oil blockade is upending even basic medical care.

item.title: Why was a £1.5bn Chinese plan to invest in a Highland yard blocked?
item.description: Ming Yang proposed building a wind turbine manufacturing plant at Ardersier.

item.title: Israel says it has killed Iran's navy chief overseeing Strait of Hormuz blockade
item.description: Israel's defence minister said a number of other "senior Navy command officials" have also been killed. Iran has not yet commented.

item.title: King's Speech to take place days after key May elections
item.description: King Charles III will attend Parliament to read out the government's legislative agenda for the coming months.

item.title: Senegal to parade Afcon trophy despite title being stripped
item.description: Senegal's Football Federation boss calls the decision to hand Morocco the Afcon title a "robbery" amid plans to parade the trophy before Saturday's friendly against Peru in Paris.

item.title: French court hands Islam scholar Tariq Ramadan 18-year jail term for rape
item.description: Ramadan, a 63-year-old former Oxford professor, denied the charges but his lawyers said he was too ill to attend the trial.

item.title: Why does the US have Iran's Kharg Island in its sights?
item.description: The tiny island is home to one of the most critical pieces of Iran's energy infrastructure.

item.title: Russia wants to limit contact with the outside world
item.description: A recent internet blackout reflects the Kremlin’s nervousness

item.title: Germany’s Social Democrats gaze into the abyss
item.description: But an electoral drubbing could open a window for national reforms

item.title: France offers some hope for defeating populists
item.description: The mainstream parties get the biggest prizes

item.title: Iran’s regime walls off the internet
item.description: The latest shutdown looks designed to be permanent

item.title: Israeli settlers are growing more violent in the West Bank
item.description: Since the war with Iran began, attacks against Palestinians have become more systematic

item.title: In the current Gulf war, water may prove as decisive as oil
item.description: The Arab states are plainly vulnerable, but so is Iran

item.title: How Chinese companies are reshaping Indonesia
item.description: They are fleeing cut-throat competition at home

item.title: Millions of Burmese struggle to find safety in Thailand
item.description: Many refugees are left exposed

item.title: China is breaking into one of the world’s weirdest car markets
item.description: An island of American motoring in Central Asia is changing fast

item.title: Brazil has a secret weapon against oil shocks
item.description: Biofuels will help the country fend off the effects of conflict in the Middle East

item.title: Mexico’s broken economy
item.description: Claudia Sheinbaum’s government is tinkering, not reforming

item.title: China’s huge pork industry is a victim of its own success
item.description: The country is awash in its favourite meat

item.title: Does the Iran war increase the risk of a Chinese attack on Taiwan?
item.description: China has reason to wait, but its window of opportunity has widened

item.title: Jiang Shengnan is the most vocal woman in Chinese politics
item.description: She is trying to push change from within the system

item.title: England has shown the world how to replace farm subsidies
item.description: A rare Brexit dividend

item.title: Far-fetched to think McSweeney faked phone theft, says PM
item.description: Messages relating to Lord Peter Mandelson's appointment as US ambassador could have been lost in the alleged theft.

item.title: Speaker urged to stop Keir Starmer avoiding MPs' questions
item.description: The public are growing tired of Sir Keir Starmer's conduct at PMQs, according to letters obtained by the BBC.

item.title: Russia bans Oscar-winning film 'Mr Nobody Against Putin'
item.description: A court alleges that the documentary promoted "negative attitudes" about the Russian government and the war in Ukraine.

item.title: Army commander convicted of Guinea stadium massacre dies in prison
item.description: Aboubacar Sidiki Diakité was convicted over the 2009 massacre in which more than 150 people were killed.

item.title: The Sudden Death of a Man Who Told Chinese Kids How to Succeed
item.description: The influencer Zhang Xuefeng was known for no-nonsense, some said cynical, advice about how to win in China’s educational rat race. He died at 41.

item.title: UK forecast to see biggest hit to growth from Iran war out of major economies
item.description: The OECD downgrades forecasts for many of the world's biggest economies due to the US-Israel war with Iran.

item.title: European Parliament gives conditional approval to EU-US trade deal
item.description: The agreement would see EU tariffs on US goods reduced to zero while US tariffs on EU imports set at 15%.

item.title: Zimbabwe says 15 nationals killed after being lured to fight for Russia
item.description: Zimbabwe says it is engaging the Russian government to repatriate bodies and bring home those still alive.

item.title: How long will Israel stay in Lebanon?
item.description: The threat of another occupation is tearing at the fabric of Lebanese society

item.title: The end of the world’s longest-running Maoist insurgency
item.description: A report from one of the Naxalites’ final redoubts

item.title: Donald Trump says he is close to a deal with Iran
item.description: Almost no one believes that talks are getting anywhere

item.title: Advantage Iran
item.description: A month of bombing has achieved nothing. Will Donald Trump escalate, or talk?

item.title: Europe should think twice before weakening its merger rules
item.description: A strict competition policy is not the barrier to bigger firms

item.title: The case against energy bail-outs
item.description: As war rages in Iran, governments must not repeat the mistakes of 2022

item.title: New India bill to amend transgender rights sparks protests
item.description: The bill seeks to change how transgender people are legally recognised and their right to self-identify.

item.title: 'We are being choked': Philippines transport workers strike over fuel costs
item.description: It comes as a ship carrying barrels of Russian crude oil arrived in the Philippines earlier this week.

item.title: Trump confirms May meeting with Xi Jinping as Iran war forces postponement
item.description: Trump's delayed meeting with Xi Jinping will be the first visit to China by a US president since 2017.

item.title: India's fertiliser supplies under strain as war disrupts shipments
item.description: Analysts say current stocks are enough for the upcoming sowing season, but this may change if the war stretches on.

item.title: What to look out for in European World Cup play-offs
item.description: The World Cup play-offs start on Thursday with four European spaces up for grabs for this summer's tournament.

item.title: In one of country's poorest states, Venezuelans hope for post-Maduro boom
item.description: Ione Wells travels to Sucre, where a lack of investment has hampered locals' prospects.

item.title: Nigel Farage hints at return for fellow Essex MP
item.description: Nigel Farage says he hopes things are "ironed out" for James McMurdock MP.

item.title: Australia's sunscreen regulator wants new rules after recent product scandal
item.description: The medicines regulator is proposing stricter rules after more than a dozen products were recalled.

item.title: Australia bans Iranian tourists with valid visas for six months
item.description: Critics say the move, which comes after Iranian footballers were granted asylum, is a "moral failure".

item.title: Ministers hope red tape cuts will speed up decision-making
item.description: The Conservatives said the changes were being made by a government "stuck in consultation paralysis".

item.title: Trump's Iran strategy is to pursue two off-ramps at once
item.description: The White House insists the president is dictating events, but even some Republicans are frustrated by mixed messaging about his war aims.

item.title: Peers defy government by pushing for UK social media ban for under-16s
item.description: The government is consulting on whether the UK should follow Australia by introducing a ban.

item.title: Iran's rejection of US talks reflects deep mistrust
item.description: This does not mean the door is shut, as Iran's tough stance may be more about setting conditions than rejecting diplomacy.

item.title: A right-wing populist party made big poll gains - and it's shaking up Australian politics
item.description: Pauline Hanson's One Nation is riding a wave of popularity driven by voters weary of mainstream parties.

item.title: Iran's foreign minister says there are no negotiations with US
item.description: Trump says he is talking to "the right people" in Iran, but Tehran says no such negotiations are being held.

item.title: UN votes to recognise enslavement of Africans as 'gravest crime against humanity'
item.description: The landmark resolution calls for an apology and contributions to a reparations fund, without specifying an amount.

item.title: Europe’s populist right should be outvoted rather than ostracised
item.description: Imagining there’s no fascists is a bad strategy

item.title: The Revolutionary Guards are taking over Iran
item.description: They now appear to run both the state and the war

item.title: Mexico must unleash its private sector
item.description: Claudia Sheinbaum’s biggest problem is weak investment and growth, not Donald Trump

item.title: Four arrested in Germany after Channel smuggling gang raids
item.description: Europol said more than 450 officers from the UK, Germany and Belgium were involved in the operation.

item.title: Senegal appeal to be heard 'as swiftly as possible'
item.description: The Court of Arbitration for Sport (Cas) says it will rule on Senegal's appeal against being stripped of the Africa Cup of Nations title "as swiftly as possible".

item.title: Saudi Pro League, the US or Europe - what are Salah's options?
item.description: Liverpool reporter Aadam Patel analyses what Mohamed Salah's next move might be following the forward's announcement that he is to leave the club this summer.

item.title: Estonia and Latvia say territories hit by stray Ukrainian drones
item.description: The incidents occurred on the same night that Ukraine launched a massive attack on Russia's port of Ust-Luga, near the Estonian border.

item.title: Indian singer's drowning was accidental - Singapore coroner
item.description: The coroner ruled that there was no evidence of foul play in the death of iconic singer Zubeen Garg.

item.title: 'There's no safety anymore': Palestinians warn of expanding West Bank settler violence
item.description: There has been a surge of attacks by Israeli settlers on Palestinian villages across the occupied territory since the start of the Iran war.

item.title: Brazil's Bolsonaro gets temporary house arrest for ill-health
item.description: Former President Jair Bolsonaro was moved from his cell to hospital earlier this month with pneumonia.

item.title: The world’s most unaffordable housing is not where you think
item.description: Asia’s great cities will founder without more reasonably priced homes

item.title: Marcos promises 'flow of oil' as Philippines declares energy emergency
item.description: The President says the country will procure one million barrels of oil to add to the current stock.

item.title: Where does Salah rank among greatest Premier League forwards?
item.description: As Mohamed Salah announces he will leave Liverpool this summer, BBC Sport asks you to rank the best forwards in Premier League history.

item.title: Has banning phones improved performance at Dutch schools?
item.description: Two years ago the Netherlands banned phones in schools. Now the government wants to go further, pushing to restrict social media for under‑16s.

item.title: Death toll from Colombian military plane crash rises to 69 as search ends
item.description: The Hercules military transport plane crashed minutes after take-off near Colombia's southern border with Peru.

item.title: What Iranians make of the possibility of talks to end the war
item.description: Some Iranians dismiss the possibility of talks to end the war, others worry a deal may leave the current leaders in place.

item.title: China’s government both drives and constrains the rise of AI
item.description: Officials want to lead the world in the new technology, but are still wary of disruption

item.title: German outcry over deep fake porn targeting actress prompts bid to change law
item.description: Collien Fernandes has accused her ex-husband of spreading sexualised images of her online.

item.title: Small window open for US-Iran talks, but swift end to war still unlikely
item.description: President Trump says the US is already dealing with a "top person" in Iran, but Tehran denies that any talks have begun.

item.title: Everyday life in Asia is being upended by Iran war fuel crisis
item.description: Asia relies heavily on oil and gas from the Gulf, and shortages and higher prices are starting to bite.

item.title: Israel says it will take control of large buffer zone in southern Lebanon
item.description: Thousands of displaced Lebanese residents will not be allowed to return home until northern Israel is safe, Israel's defence minister says.

item.title: Marco Rubio testifies in former housemate's Maduro lobbying trial
item.description: Rubio's former housemate is charged with illegally trying to influence the first Trump administration on behalf of the Venezuelan government.

item.title: Iran war shows norms of international conflicts have been overturned
item.description: US threats against energy infrastructure, and Iran's retaliation on its Gulf neighbours, signal a clear change.

item.title: Botswana prepares to take an even bigger gamble on diamonds
item.description: The African country wants to own more of De Beers, the firm that helped it to escape poverty
